speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] 1996. The survivor is Mrs. Bella Sobol. This is tape five. Mrs. Sobol, I wanted to ask you, who survived in your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: All of them got-- went to the crematorium.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your mother, what was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's was Mindel. My father was Moishe. My sister was Frida. Another sister was Esther. And I had a brother, the youngest, Abe.

speaker: interviewee
text: All died in the same place, in Birkenau in the crematorium.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. When you were raising your children, do you think you did any things differently because you are a survivor?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was always afraid. I was very protective. Any little thing, I was-- my heart, and when they did something, you know or they was-- like little children, they fight, or they fall down, or they was sick, my heart went out. I was so afraid something's going to happen to my child. And I'm going to lose my child.

speaker: interviewer
text: I was always between them when they was playing or they was fighting. I was protecting them. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] What else you want? Did you ever discuss the Holocaust with your children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, constant-- when the time they came to their age.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they-- they're-- Oh, let me see-- after bar mitzvah.

speaker: interviewee
text: A little-- a little older. Mine oldest son knows everything. He knows, he was after me-- he called me lately. And he said, Ma, I watched on the-- I watched the, the commentary. And I took down the telephone. And I even bought the video. And you don't-- and I'm going to call for you. And I'm going to arrange. I said, dear son, your mother did already. He said, I'm very proud of you. He was constantly after me to do it. I said, I'm-- I cannot sit and speak, because it's very painful for me. He said, I want you to leave something for your grandchildren. So my son, he is-- my older son, he-- they-- he knows every name. And he know every place. They were-- there's no article and book-- he was reading about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever have any nightmares?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, constantly. Yeah, terrible. They're chasing me. They're shooting at me. I'm hiding. They're, they're, they're trying to take me out from a hole. I think-- I see another people working, like I described, the little children. I see everything-- it's the things what-- it's, it's in mine memory. Nobody can just-- I can never forget this, what I saw.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you think you survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- it's a hard question to answer. I had a big will to live. I really always was saying, they're not going to get me. I was lucky. I was not sick. I tried-- I, I washed myself in the snow. I went out. I always tried not to be in their way. And, and, and secondly, just luck-- maybe I was lucky. That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else you would like to add to your testimony?

speaker: interviewee
text: I hope, you know, never going to happen again. And I hope my children and my grandchildren, grand-grandchildren, they never going to experience what their grandmother and their mother went through in their life. And I wish them a long and happy life.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to thank you for participating in this project. Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Sobol, will you please introduce your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. This is Hanka Goldberg, my only sister who survived with me the concentration, Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Bella. Oh, I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Bella Sobol, my only sister from Plonsk. This is my everything what I have.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you have really shared your life together.

speaker: interviewee
text: We shared the life, death and life. She sacrificed her life for me. If not of her, I would not survive. I was the--

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. weak one. She was the strong one. So I'm very grateful. And I miss her very much, because she lives in Connecticut. She lives in the States, and l live in Canada. And you, yeah--

speaker: interviewee
text: I live in Canada. And we're not seeing each other so often.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you're here together in Florida.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I will gladly be together with her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sure. So what's going to happen to the pictures? Mrs. Sobol, you have a tattoo on your arm? Would you please read the numbers?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you get this done?

speaker: interviewee
text: This I got in Birkenau-Auschwitz. And this was my name the whole time I was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you. Mrs. Sobol, please describe who is in this picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my father-- the name, too?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my father Moishe Kozica. This picture was taken before we went to Auschwitz. Every-- everyone needed a passport. I would like to tell a little story about this picture. When I was liberated and I came back to my hometown, I went into the city hall. In the city hall on the floor, there was hundreds and hundreds of pictures. And between all these pictures, I found a little passport picture. And this was my father.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in Plonsk, in Plonsk-- [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Sobol, please describe who is in this picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: This my sister Esther and her husband. Her husband, his name was Hamelya Hershel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm, thank you. Please describe who is in this picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the other picture is my sister in the dark coat. Her name is Frida Kozica. And she is 21 years old in this picture. Was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Is there a day? I think it is 1933.

speaker: interviewee
text: There is a year on the picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And the other people are friends hers. I cannot name. I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you. Mrs. Sobol, who is in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my daughter Minna. Do you have to say her maiden-- her family name?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in Boston, Massachusetts.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was before her-- my grandson's wedding.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you. Please describe who is in this picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: Here is my son Elliot, his wife Bet, and my grandson Zachary.

speaker: interviewee
text: Last year, last year, '95, and here in Florida.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you. Please describe who is in this picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's me and my, my sister Hanka.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken-- I'm just thinking about this. Uh, 1980-- it's now 27 years ago-- what year? '88-- 1988. The occasion was bar mitzvah, my grandson's.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Please describe this picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. In the middle, in the picture is my grandson Jeffrey and his wife Gail.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Please describe this picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my other grandson, Adam, and my nephew Harvey, my sister's son.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in May, 1995. The occasion was the wedding of my grandson.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Mrs. Sobol, who is in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my grandson Daniel. And he is three years old here.

speaker: interviewee
text: In New York, someplace on the beach-- I don't know, cannot, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Please describe who is in this picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Zachary. He is a year and a half old here.

speaker: interviewee
text: In a studio or in the house. This I don't know [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: In Staten Island in the US.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you. Please describe who is in this picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's both my grandchildren, Zachary and Daniel. And this is them in the-- my son's house, last year, '95.
